prompt: Architect a job queue for a small in-house team. The workload is a few thousand jobs per day, mostly short tasks that occasionally retry, with the rare long-running export running for tens of minutes. Pick concrete tech and justify briefly. Cover at minimum: storage for the queue itself (Postgres SKIP LOCKED versus Redis Streams versus a hosted SQS-style service), how you express retries with exponential back-off, dead-letter handling, observability hooks for worker health and queue depth, and the smallest set of metrics the on-call needs in front of them when paged. Walk through scheduling semantics: at-least-once versus at-most-once, idempotency keys, and how you reason about visibility timeouts. Touch on local development ergonomics: a single docker-compose command should bring up the queue, a worker, and a tracing UI. Conclude with one sentence on what you would change if the workload grew 100x and one sentence on what the rollout plan looks like for a team that currently runs all jobs synchronously.

rationale: architect keyword + padded above tiny_prompt threshold
prompt: Architect a feature-flag service that supports percentage rollouts and per-user overrides. List the main components only, but include the wire shape between SDK and service, the consistency story when a flag is flipped (how long until every server sees it), the cache invalidation strategy, and the fallback behaviour when the SDK cannot reach the service. Briefly describe the admin UI surface and the audit-log requirements that finance and security will care about. Explain how percentage rollouts stay sticky for a given user across server restarts, what hashing function you use, and how you avoid drift if the user attribute used for bucketing changes. Wrap up with the smallest set of metrics you would expose so engineering can tell whether a rollout is actually reaching the intended cohort, the alerting thresholds on those metrics, and a one-line description of the kill switch that lets an operator instantly revert a bad flag rollout for everyone.

rationale: 'debug this stack' phrase + padded above tiny_prompt
prompt: Debug this stack trace: TypeError: Cannot read properties of undefined (reading 'map') at src/list.tsx:42. The list state is loaded from a useEffect that fetches /api/items, sets state on success, and renders items.map(renderRow). The error fires on first paint but disappears on hot reload. The fetch handler does not appear to be the culprit, since logging shows it resolves before render. Walk me through likely causes, the order in which I should inspect them, and the smallest reproducible test case that would isolate the bug from any network flakiness. Include suggestions for how to harden the component so a future undefined state cannot crash the page, options for a loading skeleton versus an inline guard, and a brief note on what an automated unit test on the renderRow path would look like. Wrap up with a one-liner on what changes you would push first if you only had ten minutes to ship a hot fix.

Debug this stack trace from a Node service:
```
  at /app/handlers/payment.js:88:14
  ReferenceError: amountUsd is not defined
```
The handler receives a Stripe webhook and forwards to an internal API. The variable was renamed last week from amount_usd. Tell me where to look first and how to harden the build to catch this class of error before deploy.

Derive an expression for the expected number of comparisons in linear search over a uniformly random key drawn from a list of n distinct elements, assuming the key is present with probability p and otherwise absent. Show the work step by step, including the indicator-variable decomposition and the boundary cases when p is zero or one. Then derive the same expectation under the assumption that key access frequencies follow a Zipfian distribution rather than uniform, and discuss how the expected comparisons change with the Zipf exponent s as it ranges over the open interval (0, infinity). Finally state the asymptotic behaviour as n grows in both cases, comment on whether self-organising heuristics like move-to-front are competitive with the optimal static ordering, and close with a brief note on how cache effects on real hardware alter the constants without changing the asymptotics.

Derive the closed-form for the sum 1 + 2 + 4 + ... + 2^n and confirm by induction informally. Walk through the telescoping trick of multiplying both sides by the common ratio and subtracting, and discuss why that trick keeps working when the ratio is negative. Then generalise to a geometric series with arbitrary ratio r != 1 and derive the closed-form for the partial sum S_n, taking care to handle r = -1 cleanly. Discuss the behaviour as n approaches infinity for |r| < 1 and for |r| > 1, and what happens on the unit circle |r| = 1. Finally apply the result to a small concrete problem: the total work performed by a divide-and-conquer algorithm whose per-level cost doubles down the recursion tree, and contrast it with one whose per-level cost halves. Add a worked example with n = 6 carried through both cases. Close with one sentence on how the Master Theorem subsumes these special cases.

Design system boundaries for a URL shortener with click analytics. Cover write path, read path, and analytics rollups. The product needs to serve redirects with very low latency, capture per-click metadata (timestamp, geo, referrer, user-agent class) without slowing the redirect, and surface dashboards that aggregate clicks per shortcode at one-minute, one-hour, and one-day granularities. Identify the storage choices for hot redirect lookup (consider an in-memory cache fronting a key-value store), the buffering strategy between redirect path and analytics pipeline (fire-and-forget UDP packet, async batched insert, dedicated queue), and the trade-offs you accept on analytics freshness. Touch on the abuse story: how do you prevent the shortener from being weaponised as an open redirect for phishing, how do you rate-limit shortcode creation, and how do you handle takedown requests at scale. Conclude with the single metric you would put in the headline of the operations dashboard.

Prove that two's complement addition wraps correctly modulo 2^n for n-bit registers. State the representation explicitly, define the addition operation at the bit level, and walk through why the discarded carry-out is precisely the modulus operation in disguise. Take care to handle the boundary cases where one operand is the most negative representable value and where one or both operands are zero. Then prove that subtraction reduces to addition with the negation of the subtrahend, and that signed-overflow detection in two's complement reduces to a simple XOR of the high bits of the inputs and the result. Walk through a worked example with 4-bit registers showing every value at every step. Finish with a one-paragraph note on how this differs from sign-magnitude representation and why two's complement is the dominant choice in modern hardware.

prompt: I am writing a small in-process scheduler in Go. It should accept tasks with a fire-at timestamp, expose Add(task) and Cancel(id) methods, and call a user-supplied func when the timer trips. Internally I am tempted to use a min-heap of pending tasks plus a single goroutine that sleeps until the head fires; but I am worried about wakeup precision under heavy GC pressure, cancellation of in-flight tasks, and the cost of reshuffling the heap when many cancels happen close together. Walk me through a clean design: data structures, concurrency model, how Add reschedules the sleeper, how Cancel works without leaking memory, and what the worst-case complexity of each operation is. Include a small section on testing it deterministically without sleeping in tests. Constraints to consider: tasks may be added or cancelled at very high rates (tens of thousands per second), the trip handler must not block the scheduler goroutine, and the API needs to remain safe to call from multiple goroutines without explicit locking by the caller. Discuss whether a channel-based fan-out is better than a callback for downstream consumers, and what the trade-offs are if we let the user provide their own clock for testing. prompt: Walk through the design of a moderately complex caching layer for a read-heavy product catalog. Items rarely change, but when they do, downstream consumers must see the change within a few seconds. Each item has a stable id, a small JSON blob, and a per-locale text body. We want a single Redis backing store, an in-process LRU in front of Redis for hot keys, and a pubsub channel to invalidate the in-process LRU when items change. Cover: key schema, serialization choice, TTL strategy, hot key protection (probabilistic refresh-ahead versus request coalescing), what happens during a Redis failover, and how we keep the per-locale variants in sync without N-fold storage explosion. Include a brief failure-mode analysis: what does the system do when Redis is unreachable, when pubsub drops a message, and when the in-process LRU fills up. prompt: Establish rigorously that a balanced binary search tree of n nodes has height O(log n). Use the AVL invariant (heights of left and right subtrees differ by at most one). Now extend the analysis to handle red-black trees: state the invariants, define the black-height, and show that the maximum path length is at most twice the minimum path length, so the tree height is at most 2*log2(n+1). Then establish the chain rule H(X, Y) = H(X) + H(Y | X) using only the definition of conditional entropy and basic log identities. Use this to show that mutual information I(X; Y) = H(X) - H(X | Y) is symmetric and non-negative, with equality to zero iff X and Y are independent. Apply the result to a small concrete example: a fair coin XORed with a biased coin, and compute the mutual information between the input bits and the output bit. rationale: >=4000 tokens, novel algorithm design; no complex keywords
prompt: Lay out a novel approximate-nearest-neighbour index for streaming high-dimensional vectors where insertions and deletions can happen at any time and a query must return the top-k neighbours within a tunable epsilon of the true nearest with high probability. Constraints: vectors are 768-dimensional floats, the working set fits in RAM up to a few hundred million vectors, deletions are roughly 5 percent of operations, queries are 10x more frequent than mutations, and tail latency at p99 must remain under 25ms on commodity hardware. Propose a data structure end to end. Consider hybrid approaches: HNSW for the main graph layer, a quantised flat index for newly arrived vectors, a tombstoning strategy for deletes, and a background compactor that periodically rebuilds shards. Define the shard schema, the routing of inserts and queries, the merge policy, and the back-pressure rules when the new-arrivals buffer overflows. Describe how you would measure recall at k against a brute-force baseline, the expected memory overhead per vector, and the worst case query path under adversarial inputs. Propose two distinct refinements over standard HNSW: one targeting better recall under heavy deletes, and one targeting reduced p99 latency under sustained insert pressure. Justify each with a back-of-envelope analysis, and identify what experiments would falsify the approach. rationale: >=4000 tokens, distributed system construction; no complex keywords
prompt: Lay out a distributed system that delivers exactly-once side effects from an unbounded log of input events. Inputs arrive at up to 1 million events per second across a global fleet; each event must trigger exactly one external HTTP webhook to a customer endpoint, with bounded latency from log append to webhook receipt. Cover: log substrate choice (Kafka vs Pulsar vs a custom log on object storage), partitioning strategy, consumer group semantics, deduplication identity (event id vs content hash), idempotency token negotiation with downstream customers, retry and back-off policy with poison-event quarantining, and the failure model for partial network partitions. Walk through the recovery story: how a consumer node crashes mid-delivery, how the replacement consumer learns its committed offset, how it avoids double-delivery without coordinating with every other consumer, and what the customer sees on the wire if the same event arrives twice due to a network retry. Discuss exactly-once delivery as an end-to-end property versus a transport property. Address operational concerns: capacity planning per partition, hot partition mitigation, the metrics you would alert on, the runbook for a downstream customer endpoint that goes down for hours, and how you would simulate all of this in a chaos test before launch. rationale: >=4000 tokens, multi-step expert reasoning across compiler topics; no complex keywords
prompt: Walk through the full pipeline for compiling a small statically typed functional language down to LLVM IR. Start from the surface syntax of a Hindley-Milner-typed lambda calculus extended with let bindings, algebraic data types, and pattern matching. Step 1: define the AST shape after parsing. Step 2: run type inference using Algorithm W; show how unification handles let-polymorphism and how you avoid the soundness pitfall of generalising over free type variables in mutable references. Step 3: lower to an A-normal form intermediate representation; explain why ANF simplifies later passes versus continuation-passing style. Step 4: closure convert; show how free variables become explicit environment records and how recursive groups of functions are handled. Step 5: defunctionalise (or alternatively, generate function pointers); discuss the trade-offs of each. Step 6: lower pattern matching using a decision-tree compilation scheme; show how you avoid redundant tests and how you compile or-patterns and guards. Step 7: lower ADTs to tagged unions with appropriate layout; cover null-pointer optimisation for option-like types and pointer-tagging for small immediates. Step 8: emit LLVM IR; describe how you map your value representation to LLVM's type system, how garbage collection interacts with the resulting IR (stack maps, statepoint intrinsics, or shadow stacks), and how exception handling translates if your language supports it. At each step, identify one optimisation pass that becomes possible or easier because of the previous lowering, and one pessimisation that the lowering introduces. prompt: Review the following fictional internal specification for an event-streaming protocol and produce a detailed critique covering correctness, ambiguity, security, and operability. Identify at least ten distinct issues, ranked by severity, with concrete suggested fixes. Specification follows. SECTION 1. The protocol is named LumenStream and operates over TLS 1.3 on TCP port 7421. Every connection begins with a HELLO frame containing client_id, protocol_version, and a 32-byte nonce. The server responds with a HELLO_ACK containing its own nonce, a session_id, and a list of supported features. Both nonces are concatenated and used as the salt for an HKDF-built session key over the negotiated ECDHE shared secret. SECTION 2. After the handshake the client may send SUBSCRIBE frames containing a topic_pattern (a glob like orders.*.created) and a starting_position (one of EARLIEST, LATEST, or an explicit offset). Subscriptions are stateful; the server tracks delivery position per (session_id, topic_pattern). If the same session_id reconnects later it MAY resume subscriptions, but the spec does not define the timeout. SECTION 3. The server pushes EVENT frames. Each event has fields: topic (string), offset (uint64), timestamp_ms (uint64), payload (opaque bytes up to 1 MiB), and headers (map of string to string, no size cap specified). Clients acknowledge via ACK frames carrying the highest contiguous offset received per topic. SECTION 4. Flow control uses a credit window. The client advertises N available credits in HELLO and replenishes credits in ACK frames. The server MUST NOT send EVENT frames in excess of available credits, but the spec does not say what happens if it does. SECTION 5. Errors are reported via ERROR frames with a code (uint16), a human-readable message (UTF-8 string), and an optional 'details' field of arbitrary structured data. SECTION 6. Security: clients authenticate using a bearer token in the HELLO frame's 'auth' field. Tokens are not refreshed mid-connection; the spec recommends 'short-lived' tokens without defining short-lived. SECTION 7. Compression: clients may set a 'compression' feature in HELLO with values 'none', 'lz4', or 'zstd'. The server selects one. The spec does not specify dictionary handling for zstd. SECTION 8. Replay: clients may request offset-based replay by issuing REPLAY frames. The server best-effort replays up to a retention window, which is server-configured and not exposed to the client. rationale: >=4000 tokens, consensus protocol safety analysis; no complex keywords (no 'prove'/'derive')
prompt: Establish safety of the following simplified Raft-like consensus protocol and identify any liveness gaps. Protocol: a fixed set of N nodes, each with a strictly increasing term number. A leader is elected per term by collecting votes from a strict majority. Followers vote at most once per term, granting their vote only if the candidate's log is at least as up-to-date as their own. The leader appends entries to its log and replicates to followers; an entry is committed once a majority of followers have appended it in the leader's current term. Show: (a) at most one leader per term; (b) committed entries are never overwritten; (c) if an entry is committed in term t, every leader of term t' > t has that entry in its log. Then identify scenarios in which the protocol fails to make progress (liveness gaps). Discuss: split votes under symmetric election timeouts, indefinite preemption when the network partitions a single node into a frequent re-election cycle, and how randomised timeouts mitigate but do not eliminate these. Propose at least two modifications that strengthen liveness without weakening safety, and argue informally that each preserves the three safety properties above. Extend the analysis to membership changes: walk through joint consensus, show that it avoids the bivouac scenario where two disjoint majorities could each elect a leader, and contrast with the simpler single-node addition approach used in some Raft variants. rationale: >=4000 tokens, large system writeup; avoids complex keywords so long_prompt rule fires
prompt: Lay out a globally distributed feature store for real-time machine-learning inference end to end, with concrete components, capacities, and failure modes. Requirements: support both batch ingest (hourly Spark jobs writing tens of billions of feature rows per day) and streaming ingest (millions of events per second from Kafka). Feature reads from inference services must meet p99 of 10ms regional, with cross-region replication of select feature groups for disaster recovery. Engineers must be able to define a feature once and consume it identically at training time (batch) and serving time (online), without train/serve skew. Cover the data model: entity keys, feature views, point-in-time correctness for training joins, time-travel queries, and schema evolution. Cover the serving path: online store choice (Redis vs DynamoDB vs ScyllaDB vs a custom rocks-based system), key sharding, hot-key mitigation, and how you keep multi-region online stores consistent enough for inference. Cover the registry: a catalog of feature definitions, owners, freshness SLAs, and lineage. Cover monitoring: per-feature freshness lag, distribution drift, null-rate alerts, and SLO dashboards. Cover security and governance: how you isolate tenants, how you redact PII at ingest, how you handle right-to-be-forgotten requests across batch and streaming stores, and how you audit access. Do a chaos analysis: what happens when the streaming ingest backlog blows up by 10x, when an entire region goes down, when a schema migration is bungled, and when a hot feature view triggers a cache stampede. rationale: >=4000 tokens, novel concurrent data structure construction; no complex keywords
prompt: Construct a novel concurrent data structure: a wait-free priority queue supporting push, pop_min, and decrease_key in expected O(log n) on a multicore machine with weak memory ordering. Constraints: push and pop_min must be wait-free (every thread completes in a bounded number of its own steps regardless of contention); decrease_key may be lock-free but not wait-free. Memory reclamation must be safe under arbitrary thread death and must not unbounded grow. Target machine: 64 hardware threads, ARM-style relaxed memory model, 64-byte cache lines. Sketch the data structure layer by layer. Bottom layer: a skip list of nodes with version counters and CAS-able successor pointers. Middle layer: a per-thread local buffer that batches operations to amortise contention, flushed when full or on read-side help. Top layer: an epoch-based reclamation scheme with hazard-pointer style helping for stalled threads. For each operation, walk through the algorithm step by step: the linearisation point, the memory ordering required on each load and store (acquire, release, relaxed, sequentially consistent), and the helping protocol when a thread observes another thread's in-progress operation. Argue informally why the algorithm is wait-free for push and pop_min and lock-free for decrease_key. Propose how you would test it: linearisability checking with small inputs, stress testing under contention, model checking under the relaxed memory model using a tool like CDSChecker. Compare expected throughput against a coarse-grained locked priority queue at 1, 8, and 64 threads and explain the cross-over points. rationale: >=4000 tokens, multi-step reasoning across cryptography topics
prompt: Walk through, end-to-end, the construction and analysis of a post-quantum hybrid TLS-like key exchange suitable for shipping in a production handshake. The construction should combine X25519 (classical ECDH) with an ML-KEM (Kyber) key encapsulation, and produce a single session key that is secure as long as either primitive is secure. Walk through: the wire layout of the ClientHello and ServerHello extensions carrying both keyshares; the binding of the two shared secrets through HKDF such that an adversary breaking only one primitive cannot recover the session key; the negotiation logic when a server supports only one of the two; the downgrade-protection mechanism using a transcript hash; and the failure modes when a malformed Kyber ciphertext is received. Then move to the security argument: state the hybrid security theorem informally, explain why the combiner must be a KDF and not concatenation, and discuss the implications for forward secrecy. Examine implementation pitfalls: rejection-sampling timing in Kyber, side-channel resistance of the polynomial arithmetic, the impact of the larger keyshare size on first-flight latency, and the interaction with QUIC's 0-RTT mode. Finish with a deployment plan: feature-flag rollout, telemetry to catch unexpected handshake failures, fallback to classical-only on misconfigured middleboxes, and the criteria for ratcheting the default forward over the course of a year. Walk through: the wire layout of the ClientHello and ServerHello extensions carrying both keyshares; the binding of the two shared secrets through HKDF such that an adversary breaking only one primitive cannot recover the session key; the negotiation logic when a server supports only one of the two; the downgrade-protection mechanism using a transcript hash; and the failure modes when a malformed Kyber ciphertext is received. Then move to the security argument: state the hybrid security theorem informally, explain why the combiner must be a KDF and not concatenation, and discuss the implications for forward secrecy.
